events.content: A missing manifest. A client with no name. Thirty years of silence, preserved in the gap between two relay pings.

Transit Node Theta-9. Three docking rings, one functioning. A vending unit with a cracked housing cycles through a menu for food that hasn't been stocked in eight years. The node smells of recycled atmosphere and old decisions. A job terminal blinks amber in the corner. Someone left a message.

Ring 7 is the only dock still pressurised. A hauler with no registry markings sits dark in the bay — nobody's asking, nobody's answering. The smell here is different: metal, cold, something oily underneath. Mira runs a table near the airlock. She deals in whatever the manifest doesn't mention.

The wreck field starts here, where navigation gets advisory. Chunks of hull plate and frozen atmosphere drift in loose formation. The oldest debris has been here long enough to collect a thin skin of microcrystals — it catches your suit lamp and scatters it. Somewhere deeper, a beacon still pings. It has been pinging, unacknowledged, for thirty years.

The ship is a Gerrikson-class colony transport. Or was. The hull designation has been scored off with something hot — not corrosion, not collision. Done deliberately, done carefully. The breach on the starboard side is old enough that the edges have been rounded by microimpact erosion. Something is still running inside. The beacon. Whatever runs the beacon.

The interior still has partial pressure — someone sealed it at some point, or it sealed itself. Emergency lighting only. The passenger bay is empty: berths made up, personal lockers open and cleared. Not abandoned in a hurry. Cleared. Methodically. The flight recorder housing is cracked but the unit is intact. A small steady light confirms it is still recording.

The port authority stopped staffing this annex in 2278. What's here now is Sable: technically a contractor, technically licensed, technically not an obstacle. She sits behind the counter like she grew there. The door behind her is marked ARCHIVE — RESTRICTED ACCESS. The lock requires a maintenance code. She is the maintenance code.

tags: The door requires a maintenance access card.
events.content: A small room. One terminal, one chair, one overhead light that buzzes at 60Hz. The terminal is active. Records going back forty years, unfiled, unaudited. The uplink port in the corner has a cable run to somewhere — the cable disappears into the wall and the wall has no label. Somebody has been maintaining this room even though it officially doesn't exist.

The Drift Market operates in the node's former cargo bay. Stalls made from cargo containers, crates, and repurposed equipment housing. Barter mostly, some hard currency. The noise level says commerce; the eyes of the vendors say something else. Everybody here is waiting for something to stop happening.

Hektor's stall is three crates and a lamp. The inventory is spread on a piece of cargo sheeting: connectors, coils, signal components, one extremely old handheld unit whose function nobody has been able to determine in fifteen years. Hektor doesn't talk much. He is here because the other option is somewhere he can't go back to.

The maintenance bay is the most honest place on the node. Everything here has a function, every function is legible, every part either works or it doesn't. A workbench runs the length of one wall. The hatch in the floor is labelled COLD STORAGE in stencil that's been partially painted over. The paint is newer than the stencil.

Cold Storage. Temperature: 4 Celsius. The shelves are empty except for a laminated cargo manifest taped to the wall and a storage log clipped to a hook. Whatever was here has been moved. The hook for the log says CURRENT MANIFEST. The manifest on the wall is dated two months ago and shows the space as empty. The log says something different.

tags: The pressurised walkway to Docking Ring 7.
EVA lock. Suit up before exiting.
The corridor to the port authority annex.
The lock panel blinks red. No access.
The cargo bay. The Drift Market.

events.content: An encrypted job ticket. Client: ANONYMOUS. Target: cargo manifest GX-7714, departure 2291, origin Theta-9. Fee: 4,000 sats on delivery. No questions. No contact. Payment routed through three dead relays.
A handheld spectrograph. Reads material composition and — importantly — reveals heat-treated surfaces, including deliberately scored hull designations. Battery good. Calibration questionable.
A compact contraband scanner with Mira's serial tag scratched off the casing. She wants this back. The kind of want that comes with an implication.
High-tolerance signal relay coil. The kind used in deep-space boosters before the newer compression standard made them obsolete. Hektor has two. He sold you one.
Emergency power cell, still at 80% charge. Standard issue for Gerrikson colony transports. Thirty years old. Whoever designed it built it to last.

A field-assembled signal booster. Relay coil plus power cell, wired correctly. It will amplify a degraded signal enough to extract structured data from a cracked flight recorder housing. Good for one read.
A maintenance access card for Theta-9 Port Authority Archive systems. Sable's signature on the back. She didn't sign it willingly but she signed it.
Hull registry designation recovered via spectrograph scan. The scoring was thorough but heat-treatment leaves a trace. GX-7714. Gerrikson series. Colony transport class.

Departure timestamp extracted from flight recorder: 2291.047. Day 47 of the year 2291. The recorder shows a departure from Theta-9. The official Theta-9 departure log for 2291.047 shows no departures.

Passenger class designation from the archived manifest record: INDENTURED. A labour contract classification. Standard for colony transit in the 2280s. Not standard: the contract terms appended to this record, which are not standard colony labour agreements. They are something else.
A standard job terminal. The amber light indicates unread message. The sender field is blank.

tags: The locker opens. The spectrograph is inside.
events.content: A heavy equipment locker bolted to the dock wall. Mira's lock — her own hardware, not the node's.

Fragments from multiple vessels — this isn't one wreck, it's an accumulation. The Gerrikson transport is the largest mass. Something smaller hit it, or was hit by it. The scar angles suggest impact from inside the larger hull.

tags: The spectrograph resolves the heat trace. Letters emerge.
The designation has been physically scored off. You'd need a spectrograph to recover the heat trace.
events.content: Where the hull designation plate should be, there is a rectangular scar. Done with something hot. Done on purpose.

tags: The booster amplifies the signal. Data streams across your visor.
The recorder housing is cracked — the signal is too degraded to read without amplification.
events.content: The flight recorder housing is cracked along the seam. The status light is green — it's recording, it's intact — but the output signal is too degraded to read without amplification.
One hundred and forty berths. All made up. All emptied. Personal storage lockers open, drawers pulled, surfaces wiped. Not abandoned — cleared. There is a difference. Someone came after.

The archive terminal is active. It holds transit records for Theta-9, unindexed. The filing system is a cipher of its own — records organised not by date or vessel but by a classification code that doesn't match any standard the client gave you.
A proper workbench: vise, tool rack, adequate light. Someone maintains this. The surface is clean.
A board covered in hand-printed notices. Jobs, warnings, things for sale that aren't listed anywhere official.
A laminated cargo manifest. Dated two months ago. Shows Cold Storage as empty. The lamination is new. The data beneath it is not.

The job was posted from a dead relay — a relay that went offline in 2293. Someone is routing messages through a node that officially doesn't exist anymore. That's not a technical error. That's intentional infrastructure.
The locker has a faded stencil under Mira's hardware: PORT AUTHORITY — TECHNICAL EQUIPMENT. This was official kit. She acquired it through a channel that isn't listed on her trader's licence.
The scoring is too deliberate for salvage. Salvagers remove what's valuable. Someone removed what was *identifying*. There's a difference between stripping a hull and erasing it.

The beacon signal is thirty-one years old by emission signature. It has been pinging on a distress frequency. Thirty-one years on a distress frequency means nobody who could respond chose to. Or the people who could respond were the people it was distressing about.
One hundred and forty berths, cleared after the ship stopped. Whatever happened to the passengers, it wasn't an accident. Accidents leave mess. Whoever did this had time and a reason to be thorough.

tags: The data resolves. A departure timestamp. Navigation logs truncated after six hours. The destination field: REDACTED.
events.content: Flight recorder output. Departure: 2291.047. Origin: Theta-9. Navigation log active for six hours and fourteen minutes after departure, then truncated. Destination field overwritten. Whatever the ship was heading for, someone removed it from the record after the fact — from inside the recorder, after it was already in the wreck.

The archive classifies records by passenger contract type. Standard designations: CITIZEN, CONTRACTED, BONDED. There is a fourth designation in the index that doesn't appear in any public registry standard: INDENTURED. It has more records than the other three combined. The records are locked behind a classification code. The code is described only as 'the word for what we don't call it.'
The uplink cable is active — there's a handshake signal at 0.3 second intervals. Someone on the other end is polling this terminal. Not querying it. Just checking if it's still there.
Most notices are ordinary. One isn't: a hand-printed card that reads LOOKING FOR RECORDS — GERRIKSON GX SERIES — ANY CONDITION — SERIOUS BUYER. No name. A relay address. The card is not new.

The storage log shows an entry from three weeks ago: 47 units, CLASSIFIED CARGO, outbound transfer to relay point 9-ECHO. Signed by a port authority contractor — the signature matches Sable's transit ID. The laminated manifest on the wall was put up after that entry. It shows the space as empty. It was put up to cover the log.

Cassian offered to help you access the archive through 'official channels' — meaning he would route the request through a guild liaison and you would receive a sanitised record, cleared for public access. That is: you would get the version of the truth the guild wants you to have. He didn't lie. That's exactly what he was offering.

The flight recorder data streams, partially corrupted. A timestamp field repeats in the noise: DEPARTURE — [YEAR].[DAY]. The year resolves clearly: 2291. The day field is fragmented. You have the flight log — find the departure day. Enter the full timestamp in format YYYY.DDD.

Sable looks at you for a long time. 'Prove you're licensed. Not by the authority — they don't license what you're actually doing. Prove you understand what you're walking into.' She slides a card across the counter, face down. 'What do you call a promise that was never meant to be kept, made to people who had no choice but to believe it?'
The archive terminal prompts for the classification code. The index says: 'the word for what we don't call it.' The INDENTURED records are locked behind this word. Enter the classification.

tags: You need the hull registry fragment.
You need the departure timestamp fragment.
You need the passenger class fragment.
events.content: Three fragments. The manifest is sealed with a derived key: SHA256 of hull registry + departure timestamp + passenger class, concatenated in that order, no separators. Combine what you know.

tags: The key resolves. The manifest opens.
events.content: The manifest decrypts.

CARGO MANIFEST — GX-7714
DEPARTURE: 2291.047, THETA-9
PASSENGER CLASS: INDENTURED
CONTRACT TYPE: LABOUR CONVEYANCE — INDEFINITE TERM
DESTINATION: REDACTED

PASSENGER LIST — 140 NAMES:

1. ARVEN, TASSEL — F — CONTRACT REF: IX-0041
2. BREK, OSUMO — M — CONTRACT REF: IX-0042
3. CALLIS, PREEN — F — CONTRACT REF: IX-0043
4. DOVAN, RIKE — M — CONTRACT REF: IX-0044
[...137 further entries...]

---
NOTE (handwritten, different ink):
If you are reading this, the key survived.
That means the fragments survived.
That means the ship survived.
Find them.
— K.
A man in a clean suit who has no visible reason to be in a transit node this far out. He is not doing nothing — he is doing nothing in a way that takes effort.
A compact woman with a trader's stillness. She watches the docking ring entrance more than she watches her merchandise.
A woman who has been behind that counter long enough that the counter seems to belong to her more than the port authority ever did.

A broad man who moves components with the careful attention of someone who understands what they're worth. He has a scar on his left hand shaped like a hull breach seal — the kind you get pressing your hand against a failing airlock.

tags: He notices you noticing him. 'Signal tracer?' A pause. 'I can see the kit. I can also help — the archive has an official request pathway. Slower, but cleaner. Guild-verified records. No liability on your end.'

'I represent clients who have an interest in accurate records. Historical records. The kind that sometimes get... misfiled.' He says this as if it explains something. It does, but not what he intends it to.
'The same people who built this node.' He lets that sit. 'They've been good to the node. They'd like to remain so.'
'I file the request, you get the record in seventy-two hours. Certified copy. Legally clean.' He is telling the truth. That is the worst thing about him.

'Good.' He files something on his handheld. 'Seventy-two hours. I'll find you.' He doesn't say what the record will contain. He doesn't have to. You understand, a few hours later, what 'certified copy' means here.

He looks at you without expression. 'You went around me.' It isn't a question. It isn't an accusation either. It's the tone of someone noting a fact for the record. 'Careful what you do with what you found.'
'You're not here to buy.' She looks at the case file if you're holding it. 'Job terminal job. Those always end badly. What do you need?'

'Port authority kit. I acquired it.' She doesn't elaborate. 'I'll trade the access. I had a scanner go missing in the wreck field three months ago. Compact unit, serial scratched off. Find it, bring it back, the locker's open to you.'
Ask why she wants it back
'Because it has a job log on it that I'd prefer not to have read by whoever finds it first.'

'Used to be a transit hub. Before the lanes shifted. Now it's whatever people need it to be.' A pause. 'The guild owns the infrastructure. That means they own what the infrastructure sees. Keep that in mind.'

'The Interstellar Shipping Guilds. They've been here since before the node had a number. They'll be here after.' She moves something on her table. 'The wreck out there — that's a guild ship. Or was. Before it stopped being one.'
She sees the scanner before you offer it. 'You found it.' She takes it, doesn't check the log in front of you. Slides a key across the table. 'Locker's yours.'

'Archive access requires a maintenance licence.' She doesn't look up. 'You don't have one. I can tell.' A pause. 'I might be able to help. If you can tell me what you're actually looking for and why it matters.'

She listens. When you finish she is quiet for a moment. 'GX-7714. I wondered when someone would come asking.' Another pause. 'I've been the reason nobody could come asking for fifteen years. That's on me.' She straightens. 'I'll give you the card. But first you answer something for me.'

'I know that whatever's in that archive has been there for thirty years and nobody with the clearance to read it has wanted to.' Her voice is level. 'I know that three contractors before me found out what it was and left the node. I stayed.' No explanation of why.
She slides the puzzle card across the counter.

'You got in.' She doesn't ask what you found. 'Whatever you do with it — that's yours. I've been sitting on it long enough.' She goes back to her paperwork. You understand she is not going to leave. She has decided that this is where she stays, and why.
'Old standard. Still works better than the new compression coils in deep field.' He moves one from a pile. '300 sats.' He doesn't barter. The price is the price.
He hands you the coil. Transaction complete.

A long pause. 'I don't talk about the node.' Another pause. 'The wreck has been out there the whole time I've been here. Twelve years. Nobody goes near it. Officially.' He looks at your kit. 'Unofficially, you'll need a booster for that recorder.'
Ask how he knows about the recorder
'I've been in the wreck. Once. Eight years ago. I left what I found there and I haven't been back.' He goes back to sorting components. End of conversation.

events.content: The certified copy arrives seventy-two hours later. It shows GX-7714 as a standard cargo run, manifest filed and closed, no passengers. The original record in your case file has been replaced. You need to find a backup terminal if the job still matters to you.

The storage log entry: three weeks ago, 47 units, CLASSIFIED CARGO, outbound to relay point 9-ECHO. Signed by a port authority contractor. The signature matches Sable's transit ID. Whatever was here, she moved it. And then covered the log with a new manifest.
Mira takes the scanner without ceremony. The locker key slides across the table.

tags: You need all three manifest fragments.
You need all three manifest fragments.
You need all three manifest fragments.
events.content: A compact data decoder terminal bolted to the archive wall. It accepts fragment inputs and derives composite keys. The kind of tool that only exists because someone needed it to exist.
